summary: On the iPad's 10th anniversary, Gruber argues that while the hardware has been excellent, the software—particularly the multitasking model—has fundamentally failed to live up to the device's revolutionary potential. He contrasts the iPad's trajectory with the Mac and iPhone, both of which were genuinely revolutionary, while the iPad has merely been a 'spectacular success.' The core problem is iPadOS's split-screen multitasking, which he calls incoherent: more complex than the Mac's windowing system yet far less capable. Gruber contends that Apple has made profound conceptual mistakes in iPad UI design that need to be scrapped entirely, not refined. He would personally prefer to run iPhone-style single-app iOS on his iPad Pro rather than deal with the current multitasking model.
key_points: The iPad's hardware is excellent—iPads Pro outperform MacBooks computationally—but the software has gotten lost
iPadOS multitasking is simultaneously more complex than the Mac's and less powerful, an especially bad combination
Split-screen multitasking uses an incoherent interaction model where launching a second app requires a completely different gesture than launching the first
The original iPad shared the iPhone's simplicity and clarity; multitasking additions destroyed that without providing Mac-level capability
Apple needs to recognize these as fundamental conceptual mistakes requiring replacement, not incremental refinement
money_quotes: The iPad has been a spectacular success, and to tens of millions it is a beloved part of their daily lives, but it has, to date, fallen short of revolutionary.
iPad multitasking: more complex, less powerful. That's quite a combination.
So the way you launch an app in the Dock for split-screen mode is identical to the way you remove that app from the Dock.
The whole concept is not merely inconsistent, it's incoherent.
How would anyone ever figure out how to split-screen multitask on the iPad if they didn't already know how to do it?
I get a phone call from my mom once a month or so because she's accidentally gotten Safari into split-screen mode when tapping links in Mail or Messages and can't get out.
If I could go back to the pre-split-screen, pre-drag-and-drop interface I would.
Apple needs to recognize they have made profound conceptual mistakes in the iPad user interface, mistakes that need to be scrapped and replaced, not polished and refined.
key_insight: The iPad's multitasking model represents a fundamental design failure—it's more complex than the Mac yet less capable, destroying the iPhone's simplicity without gaining the Mac's power.
